summary: English Enlightenment philosopher, often called the 'father of liberalism.' Author of An Essay Concerning Human Understanding (tabula rasa, primary and secondary qualities, personal identity) and Two Treatises of Government (labor theory of property, refutation of Filmer's Patriarcha, natural rights of life, liberty, and estate). Also wrote Some Thoughts Concerning Education and A Letter Concerning Toleration. Secretary to Anthony Ashley Cooper, Earl of Shaftesbury.

works.name: An Essay Concerning Human Understanding
works.description: Locke's major epistemological work argues against innate ideas in favor of the mind as a tabula rasa (blank slate) at birth. It distinguishes between simple ideas (from sensation) and complex ideas (formed by reflection), and between primary qualities (intrinsic to objects, like extension) and secondary qualities (subjective, like color and sound). The chapter 'Of Identity and Diversity' introduces the memory theory of personal identity with the prince-and-cobbler thought experiment. Thomas Reid's 'brave officer' objection exposes a transitivity problem. Other key features include the concept of 'sortals,' the 'Abuse of Words' chapter criticizing metaphysics, and Leibniz's chapter-by-chapter refutation in New Essays.

clues.clue: tabula rasa / blank slate — argued against innate ideas; the mind at birth is empty
examples: claimed that humans are not born with innate knowledge, a view often described as a tabula rasa
his conception of the mind at birth is usually described with the phrase tabula rasa

clues.clue: primary vs. secondary qualities — primary are intrinsic (extension); secondary are subjective (color, sound, smell)
examples: distinguished between primary and secondary qualities
sound and smells were only subjective secondary qualities

clues.clue: prince-and-cobbler thought experiment — a prince's soul enters a cobbler's body; memory theory of personal identity
examples: imagining a prince and a cobbler that switch minds
wrote about a prince whose soul entered the body of a cobbler

clues.clue: Thomas Reid's 'brave officer' objection — officer remembers being flogged as a boy but forgets as a general; attacks transitivity of memory
examples: an objection imagines a brave officer who forgets being flogged as a boy when he is made a general

clues.clue: simple ideas from sensation vs. complex ideas formed by reflection; 'mixed modes' like beauty and theft
examples: distinguishes between simple ideas gained from sensation and complex ideas formed by reflection
argues ideas like beauty and theft are 'mixed modes'

clues.clue: 'Abuse of Words' chapter — criticizes metaphysics for using words without clear ideas; foreshadowed logical positivism
examples: foreshadowed logical positivists like Carnap in an essay section titled 'Abuse of Words'

clues.clue: coined the term 'sortal' for categories grouping abstract general ideas by natural similitude
examples: invented a term for a category of names that group abstract general ideas

clues.clue: Day-Man and Night-Man thought experiment — share a body but have different memories
examples: used the example of Castor and Pollux sleeping in alternating shifts

clues.clue: person defined as 'a thinking intelligent being' — a 'forensic' term
examples: defined a person as 'a thinking intelligent being' and claimed 'person' was a 'forensic' term

clues.clue: Catharine Trotter Cockburn wrote a Defence of Locke's Essay
examples: Catharine Trotter Cockburn interpreted Locke's argument in her Defence

works.name: Two Treatises of Government
works.description: Locke's major political work. The First Treatise refutes Robert Filmer's Patriarcha and its doctrine of divine right of kings. The Second Treatise argues for natural rights of 'life, liberty, and estate,' the labor theory of property (mixing labor with nature creates ownership), the social contract, and the right to revolution when the state destroys the people's property. Key concepts include the 'Lockean proviso' (acquisition must leave 'enough and as good' for others, coined by Robert Nozick), 'express' vs. 'tacit' consent, and 'in the beginning, all the world was America.' C. B. Macpherson criticized Locke's 'possessive individualism.'

clues.clue: labor theory of property — mixing labor with nature creates ownership; Nozick's tomato juice in the ocean parody
examples: property comes from mixing one's labor with nature
Robert Nozick came up with the example of a man who mixes tomato juice with the ocean

clues.clue: First Treatise refutes Robert Filmer's Patriarcha — attacked divine right of kings
examples: rebuts the doctrine of divine right of kings set forth in Patriarcha by Robert Filmer

clues.clue: natural rights of 'life, liberty, and estate/property'
examples: wrote in favor of 'life, liberty, and estate'
people have natural rights of life, liberty, and property

clues.clue: Lockean proviso — 'enough and as good' for others; term coined by Nozick in Anarchy, State, and Utopia
examples: acquisition must leave 'enough and as good for others' was called his namesake 'proviso' by Robert Nozick

clues.clue: C. B. Macpherson criticized Locke's 'possessive individualism' — a person 'is an owner of himself'
examples: criticized for promoting unrestricted accumulation by C.B. Macpherson, who condemned his classist form of 'possessive individualism'

clues.clue: 'in the beginning, all the world was America' — discussion of Genesis and the state of nature
examples: In a discussion of Genesis, this thinker wrote, 'In the beginning, all the world was America'

clues.clue: 'appeal to Heaven' — the people's right to resist a ruler who would 'enslave or destroy them'
examples: described a people's right to resist a ruler as an 'appeal to Heaven'

clues.clue: chapter on slavery — some read it as apology for Locke's role in Fundamental Constitutions of Carolina
examples: wrote that a slaveholder 'shall have absolute power and authority over his negro slave' in Fundamental Constitutions of Carolina

clues.clue: James Tully's 'workmanship' interpretation — Locke doesn't advocate capitalism, only 'custodianship'
examples: James Tully argued that this work doesn't advocate capitalism since it presents only a public-serving 'custodianship'

works.name: Other Works and General / Biographical
works.description: Locke's Some Thoughts Concerning Education builds on the tabula rasa idea and grew from letters advising Mary and Edward Clarke about their son. A Letter Concerning Toleration argues that religious tolerance is beneficial but excludes atheists. Locke was secretary to Anthony Ashley Cooper, Earl of Shaftesbury. He was attacked as Socinian for The Reasonableness of Christianity. Berkeley and Butler both critiqued his Essay. Peter Laslett's 1960 edition of Two Treatises redated the work.

clues.clue: Some Thoughts Concerning Education — builds on tabula rasa; grew from letters to Mary and Edward Clarke
examples: this book grew out of a series of letters giving advice to Mary and Edward Clarke about their son

clues.clue: A Letter Concerning Toleration — religious minorities should be tolerated; atheists should not (no divine force to compel honesty)
examples: proposed that, because it is impossible to convert people by force, you might as well keep religious minorities around

clues.clue: secretary to Anthony Ashley Cooper, Earl of Shaftesbury
examples: secretary to Anthony Ashley Cooper, Earl of Shaftesbury

clues.clue: Peter Laslett's 1960 edition redated Two Treatises a decade earlier
examples: One of this philosopher's works was dated a decade earlier than previously thought in a landmark 1960 edition by Peter Laslett

clues.clue: Damaris Masham — philosopher Locke corresponded with; moved in with her family at Oates
examples: Jonathan Edwards mocked Locke as the 'governor of the seraglio at Oates' after his correspondence with this philosopher

comprehensive_summary:
John Locke (1632-1704) is one of the most frequently clued philosophers in quizbowl, with questions spanning epistemology, political philosophy, and personal identity. The two central giveaways are the tabula rasa and Two Treatises of Government.

An Essay Concerning Human Understanding argues against innate ideas in favor of the mind as a blank slate at birth. It distinguishes simple ideas (from sensation) from complex ideas (from reflection) and primary qualities (intrinsic) from secondary qualities (subjective). The prince-and-cobbler thought experiment illustrates the memory theory of personal identity, which Thomas Reid attacked with the 'brave officer' who forgets his flogging. The 'Abuse of Words' chapter criticizes metaphysics. Locke coined the term 'sortal,' defined persons as 'thinking intelligent beings' (a 'forensic' term), and used the Day-Man/Night-Man experiment. Leibniz refuted the Essay chapter-by-chapter in New Essays; Catharine Trotter Cockburn wrote a Defence.

Two Treatises of Government refutes Filmer's Patriarcha (First Treatise) and argues for natural rights of life, liberty, and property through the labor theory (Second Treatise). The 'Lockean proviso' requires acquisition leave 'enough and as good' for others. Nozick satirized the labor theory with tomato juice poured into the ocean. Macpherson attacked Locke's 'possessive individualism.' The work distinguishes 'express' and 'tacit' consent and declares 'in the beginning, all the world was America.'

Other works include Some Thoughts Concerning Education (builds on tabula rasa, from letters to the Clarke family) and A Letter Concerning Toleration (tolerates religious minorities but not atheists). Locke was secretary to Shaftesbury and was attacked as Socinian for The Reasonableness of Christianity.

cards.front: Philosopher: argued against innate ideas in favor of the mind as a tabula rasa at birth; distinguished primary qualities (intrinsic) from secondary qualities (subjective like color and sound)
Philosopher: the prince-and-cobbler thought experiment illustrates the memory theory of personal identity; Thomas Reid's 'brave officer' exposes a transitivity problem
Work: First Treatise refutes Robert Filmer's Patriarcha; Second Treatise argues that mixing one's labor with nature creates property, subject to the 'enough and as good' proviso
Philosopher: Robert Nozick parodied this thinker's labor theory of property by imagining pouring tomato juice into the ocean; Nozick coined a 'proviso' named for this thinker
Philosopher: C. B. Macpherson attacked this thinker's 'possessive individualism,' arguing his property rights provide a 'moral foundation for bourgeois appropriation'
Philosopher: coined the term 'sortal' for categories of abstract general ideas; chapter on 'Abuse of Words' foreshadowed logical positivism by criticizing metaphysical language
Work: argues that religious minorities should be tolerated because conversion by force is impossible, but atheists should not be tolerated since nothing compels their honesty
Philosopher: defined a person as 'a thinking intelligent being' and called 'person' a 'forensic' term; used the Day-Man and Night-Man to argue the soul is not always thinking
